evidence: `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` defines many callback-only runtime structs (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeStartupOps`, etc.) and then composes them via `defaultRuntime*` functions without adding domain behavior.

`goseg/importer/importer.go` uses a very wide `importerRuntime` callback bag with grouped validation (`importer-core`, `importer-upload`, `importer-provision`) and multiple resolver layers before each operation.

`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` models startup as large callback bundles (`startupInitRuntime`, `startBackgroundServicesRuntime`) and `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go` needs extensive helper-switch wiring to override individual callbacks.

`goseg/structs/configs.go` exposes dynamic section APIs (`Section`, `UpdateSection`) using `any`, while `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go` maintains large manual field registries to map/merge sections.

Config patch behavior is split across parallel representations: pointer-based patch structs in goseg/config/config_update_schema.go (ConnectivityPatch/RuntimePatch/etc), a separate per-field registry in goseg/config/config_update_registry.go (connectivityPatchFields/runtimePatchFields/etc), and section descriptors in goseg/structs/configs.go, requiring synchronized edits across files.

Urbit config mutation and decoding logic are distributed between large type-switch decoding in goseg/structs/configs.go (UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON) and section mutator registries in goseg/config/urbit.go, creating multiple structural control points for the same domain model.

Workflow runtime files combine transition orchestration, domain validation, config mutation, and infrastructure side effects in single functions (for example runStartramToggleWithRuntime in goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go and buildToggleMinIOLinkSteps/buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps in goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go).

Test suites repeatedly implement similar global-state and filesystem setup patterns (for example GS_BASE_PATH/base path swap and fixture creation in goseg/config/config_test.go and goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go) rather than using shared scenario builders.

Best-effort mutations are handled inconsistently across runtime flows: `recreateContainerIfImageChanged` logs and suppresses `ContainerStop`/`ContainerRemove` failures (`goseg/docker/lifecycle/planner.go:67-73`), while related teardown logic in `urbitCleanDelete` accumulates and returns joined stop/delete errors (`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:382-424`).
Startram cleanup paths log deletion failures but still return success (`goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go:184-189`, `:227-230`), creating silent partial-failure outcomes.

WiFi service methods mix nil-receiver error returns and panic-prone dereferences: several methods return explicit errors for nil service (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go:39-49`, `:109-153`), but `StopWiFiInfoLoop` dereferences `service.state` without nil guards (`:128-133`).

Comparable status-not-found paths use incompatible error contracts: ad-hoc string error in docker subsystem (`goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_health.go:146`) versus sentinel-wrapped typed error in workflow runtime (`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:427-429`).

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes are being used as surrogate module APIs, adding indirection without proportional leverage.
evidence: `Runtime*Ops` types in `runtime_ops.go` split behavior into many function fields and recombine them via `defaultRuntime*` composition rather than stable cohesive interfaces.

`importerRuntime` in `importer.go` contains many unrelated responsibilities (filesystem, upload auth, workflow orchestration, docker, ship config) and requires group-based callback validation before use.
`workflowRuntime` in `operations_runtime.go` repeats the same callback-bag pattern for transition emission, urbit updates, container ops, and upload coordination.
`startup_orchestrator.go` contains broad runtime bundles and policy wrappers; tests in `startup_orchestrator_test.go` require helper switch logic just to patch one callback at a time.
findings.suggestion: Replace wide callback bags with smaller role-focused interfaces (for example, transition publisher, ship config store, container lifecycle service) and inject only the minimal interface each workflow step needs; keep one composition root per subsystem.

findings.summary: Repeated new/default/resolve/withDefaults chains create call-depth and validation churn without meaningful policy separation.
evidence: `rectify.go` has `newRectifyRuntime` -> `NewRectifyRuntime` -> `NewRectifyRuntimeWithDependencies` -> `withDefaultsRectifyRuntime` -> `resolveRectifyRuntime` -> `resolveRectifyRuntimeWithDefaults` before handlers run.

`operations_runtime.go` re-resolves runtime in multiple helper paths (`resolveWorkflowRuntime`, `resolveWorkflowRuntimeWithDefaults`, `emitWorkflowTransition`, `publishTransition`) even after a runtime is already built.

`main.go` introduces wrapper methods (`bootstrapRuntimeWith`, `resolveBootstrapRuntime`, `withDefaults`, and thin `bootstrap/startServer/runC2cCheck` methods) primarily for callback forwarding and nil checks.
`wifi_runtime_api.go` similarly layers default and override construction (`NewWiFiRuntime`, `NewWiFiRuntimeWith`, service constructor checks) while most methods directly forward into runtime methods.
findings.suggestion: Collapse each subsystem to one explicit runtime builder plus one validation boundary; pass fully-built runtimes downward and avoid re-resolving in leaf helpers.

findings.summary: The `SysConfig` section abstraction relies on `any`-typed mutation and a large registry, which weakens interface honesty and obscures domain boundaries.
evidence: `SysConfig.Section` and `SysConfig.UpdateSection` in `configs.go` expose dynamic access/mutation via `any` and descriptor dispatch rather than typed contracts.
`config_update_registry.go` compensates with extensive manual patch field specs and merge rules per section, indicating abstraction friction at the update boundary.

`defaults.go` constructs a large monolithic `SysConfig`, while consumers like `main.go` and other runtimes repeatedly snapshot individual slices of config, signaling that the top-level abstraction is too coarse.
Typed convenience wrappers (`UpdateConnectivityConfig`, `UpdateRuntimeConfig`, etc.) coexist with dynamic `any` mutation, showing split abstraction direction.
findings.suggestion: Introduce typed section stores/services (for example `ConnectivityStore`, `RuntimeStore`, `AuthSessionStore`) and replace `any` section mutation with compile-time typed patch/update APIs; generate repetitive mapping where necessary.

findings.summary: Config patch semantics are duplicated across schema structs, field registries, and section descriptors, creating a fragile multi-edit design.
evidence: goseg/config/config_update_schema.go defines many patch fields (e.g., RuntimePatch.Setup/GSVersion/Disable502 and ConnectivityPatch.EndpointURL/WgRegistered) as one source of truth.

goseg/config/config_update_registry.go redefines the same field universe with per-field key/parse/apply/merge entries (for example runtimePatchFields and connectivityPatchFields), duplicating policy wiring for each field.

goseg/structs/configs.go separately defines mutable section descriptors and typed section update paths (SysConfigSectionDescriptorList + UpdateSection), adding another structural mapping layer that must remain in sync.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single declarative field catalog (key, path, parse, merge strategy, section) and generate or derive schema/apply/merge behavior from it, so adding a field requires one edit instead of synchronized edits in multiple files.

findings.summary: Urbit config decoding and mutation routing are split across separate large mapping constructs, increasing drift risk and maintenance overhead.
evidence: goseg/structs/configs.go implements UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON as a long key switch assigning many fields manually (network/runtime/schedule/web/feature/backup concerns all in one method).
goseg/config/urbit.go defines a separate urbitSectionMutatorSpecs registry to map section enums to typed mutators; these sections mirror the embedded concerns of UrbitDocker.
Both files encode structural knowledge of Urbit subdomains independently, so introducing or reclassifying a field can require touching multiple mapping systems.
findings.suggestion: Centralize Urbit field/section metadata in one typed map (or generated descriptors) used by both decode and section mutation paths, and split concern-specific logic into smaller dedicated units.

findings.summary: Ship workflow runtime handlers mix transition composition with direct infrastructure and config side effects in the same routines.
evidence: runStartramToggleWithRuntime in goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go builds workflow steps while also making config decisions, container operations, dispatching payloads, and rebuild sequencing in one body.

buildToggleMinIOLinkSteps and buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps in goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go combine domain decisions, persistence updates, external click/docker calls, and transition emission concerns.
goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go exposes broad operation bags (many function fields grouped by different concerns), which encourages high-coupling handler functions consuming wide runtimes.
findings.suggestion: Extract pure transition planning from effect execution: create narrow use-case services per operation (e.g., network toggle, minio link, startram toggle) and keep runtime handlers as thin adapters that only invoke those services.

findings.summary: Multiple test files repeat environment/base-path and fixture bootstrapping patterns instead of reusing shared harness utilities.
evidence: goseg/config/config_test.go repeatedly saves/restores global path or env state (e.g., BasePath/GS_BASE_PATH and function seam resets) inline per test.
goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go repeats nearly identical BasePath temp setup and version_info.json fixture creation across several tests.

goseg/logger/logger_test.go and goseg/importer/importer_test.go each implement local reset/setup helpers for global mutable state (init state/upload sessions/dirs) without a shared cross-package pattern.
findings.suggestion: Define reusable test harness packages for common global-state and filesystem scenario setup (base path, env restoration, fixture version files, mutable global reset) and migrate repeated inline setup blocks to those helpers.

findings.summary: Container and service teardown flows suppress operational failures in some paths, causing success returns after partial failure.
evidence: `recreateContainerIfImageChanged` logs but does not propagate `ContainerStop` and `ContainerRemove` errors (`planner.go:67-73`), then proceeds to recreate.
Startram endpoint/cancel flows log `SvcDeleteFn` failures and continue (`startram_lifecycle_runtime.go:184-189`, `:227-230`).
In contrast, `urbitCleanDelete` in workflow runtime explicitly accumulates and returns joined stop/delete failures (`operations_runtime.go:382-424`).
findings.suggestion: Standardize teardown semantics with a shared policy: either aggregate non-fatal cleanup errors and return `errors.Join(...)`, or explicitly classify and emit best-effort-only failures via a typed warning channel. Apply the same contract across planner, startram lifecycle, and workflow delete paths.

findings.summary: WiFi runtime service exposes mixed nil-service failure modes (error returns in some methods, panic risk in others).
evidence: `IsWifiEnabled`, `ConstructWifiInfo`, `ToggleDevice`, and `ApplyCaptiveRules` explicitly return errors on nil service (`wifi_runtime_api.go:39-49`, `:109-143`).
`StopWiFiInfoLoop` dereferences `service.state` immediately with no nil guard (`wifi_runtime_api.go:128-133`), creating panic risk unlike sibling methods.
Assigned tests focus on command/runtime behavior and do not cover nil receiver behavior, leaving the contract mismatch unguarded (`wifi_test.go`).
findings.suggestion: Define one nil-receiver contract for `WiFiRuntimeService` (prefer explicit error returns), add nil/state guards to `StopWiFiInfoLoop` and `StartWiFiInfoLoop`, and add table-driven nil-behavior tests to lock the contract.

findings.summary: Status lookup failures use incompatible error types across neighboring orchestration code, weakening predictable handling.
evidence: Docker subsystem returns plain formatted text for missing status: `fmt.Errorf("%s status doesn't exist", patp)` (`subsystem_health.go:146`).
Workflow runtime models the same class as sentinel + wrapping (`errShipStatusNotFound` and `shipStatusNotFoundErr`) so callers can use `errors.Is` (`operations_runtime.go:49`, `:427-429`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared typed/sentinel error for ship-status lookup misses in orchestration layers and wrap consistently (`%w`) so retry/fallback logic can reliably branch via `errors.Is`.
